messages.author: 👑 of Puts
messages.content: you took short where i went long. My setup is retest though. Opening print is the first 1 min candle so you don't seem to have it marked correctly. I've done backtesting on this setup and in long run it works great. If it's above 1 min opening range i look for call. Below it, i look for puts. As you can see later in the day it reversed and there was a better entry with clear PT.  If you have a reversal setup you need to have clear understanding of it and play it at least from key levels (in the long run reversal never worked for me so i don't play it) But that 481.64 level i had from Wednesday low would have been a good shot at entry for reversal.

messages.author: crunchybrewski
messages.content: In my opinion, there was no trade on GOOG until way late in the day.  My system (very strict) used explicit key levels and uses OBs and FVGs just above or below as confirmation. No levels were retested until something like 2:30 P, when most of the volume has left the market. Technically you can use the double top at 12:45 bc it was testing an OB and showed weakness, but that's not in my system.  The QQQ trade had a beautiful entry a smidge after 10 (where I find the most success). It retested the 5min OR low with confirmation. The peak at 10:30 was nothing but a gap fill to continue lower, so it wouldnt have stopped my system out. As for me, I only saw one entry.

messages.author: RocketRacer
messages.content:
Hello All,
I am not sure if this is common knowledge but I was hoping to get some confirmation from those in the know. It appears that the farther out of the money you take a contract the larger the % loss you will experience on a drawdown of the underlying stock. For example, I analyzed both of the TSLA trades that Jack and Tony took today and I am not saying I had exactly the same entries and exits but if you analyze the contracts keeping the entry and exit the same I see the following:
For the first trade if you took:
$360 contract = 26% loss
$365 contract = 29% loss
$370 contract = 32% loss
$375 contract = 34% loss
$380 contract = 37% loss
$385 contract = 39% loss
$390 contract = 41% loss
$395 contract = 43% loss
$400 contract = 47% loss
$405 contract = 50% loss

messages.author: Lauren (lakatrades)
messages.content:
Good Morning Sam - 

To answer your question :  Reversal Confirmation:

I observed a strong bullish rejection around the bottom of a clear demand zone where the price had a previous imbalance. The downward trendline broke as a reversal candle formed with a long lower wick, signaling buyers stepping in.
Following the rejection, a small bullish engulfing candle further confirmed momentum shifting to the upside.
I entered the trade where I labeled "Bought Apple Contracts", as the candles began printing higher lows and the downtrend lost steam.

Stop-Loss Placement:

I placed my stop-loss below the demand zone and the wick of the rejection candle. This aligned with key support and offered a good risk-to-reward ratio while protecting against further downside if the reversal failed.

messages.author: Lauren (lakatrades)
messages.content:
<@1025807331327475863> 

Great question! Comparing your second chart and my first one, here’s how I’d approach the scenarios you outlined:

Scenario 1 (Green Arrow):
If NVDA filled the gap, broke the 5-minute opening range, retested the bottom line of the opening range, and then pushed up:

Yes, I would consider entering after the retest and confirmation of strength (e.g., a bullish candle closing above the retest level).
This setup aligns with gap-fill momentum, which often leads to a continuation if volume supports it.
Stop Loss (SL): Just below the retest level or slightly under the gap-fill zone to account for noise.
Scenario 2 (Red Arrow):
If NVDA broke the top of the 5-minute opening range, retested, and then started selling:

No, I wouldn’t enter here. The initial breakdown of the top of the range would signal weakness, especially if it couldn’t hold the breakout level.
Entering short after the breakdown could work for a different strategy, but for me, I’d wait for further confirmation that the sellers are in control before considering a trade in this direction.
Entry at 8:19 (Orange Arrow):

I entered because NVDA was forming a bullish triangle (as shown in my chart), signaling consolidation with the likelihood of a breakout. The price was respecting key levels like the premarket high and hourly resistance.
The breakout was accompanied by increasing volume, confirming strength.
SL for this trade: Below the last low of the triangle (around $138.51 on my chart), allowing the price room to breathe while managing risk effectively.
The primary difference is context—whether NVDA respects its key levels (gap-fill, opening range, premarket high) and how volume supports the move. Let me know if that helps clarify!

messages.author: Lauren (lakatrades)
messages.content:
Trade Recap – QQQ (March 6, 2025)
Morning Chop: Multiple BNRs failed, no clean setups, stayed out.
Entry: Short after break & retest of key level.
Trend Confirmation: Strong rejection, followed the downtrend.
Trade Management: Scaled out at TP1 & TP2, respected trendline.
Exit: Closed all remaining contracts when price broke the PYA (Protect Your Asset) trendline.
Key Takeaways:

✅ Avoided early chop – waited for a solid setup.
✅ Break & retest gave conviction for the short.
✅ Trendline guided trade – locked profits before reversal.

messages.author: Lauren (lakatrades)

Trade Recap – MSFT (March 6, 2025)
Strong Setup: BNR at ORH (5-min high) confirmed entry.
Entry: Long position after clean break & retest.
Momentum: Strong push up, filling premarket gap.
Trade Management: Followed PYA (Protect Your Asset) trendline.
Exit: Closed all contracts when price broke the PYA trendline.

✅ Premarket gap fill gave momentum for a strong move.
✅ PYA trendline guided the trade and secured profits.

messages.author: unnas
messages.content: I used to enter into these kind of trades when I was first learning about the break and retest strategy.  Every time I saw a good retest, I would take the trade (good thing it was only paper money back then).  The biggest lesson I learned is to develop a HTF thesis first, then enter a set up that lines up with the HTF thesis.  For example, we are currently in a downtrend and most stocks (including NVDA) are in a downtrend.  Therefore, a suggested setup would be to look for a shorting position on a key level.  I make sure to watch Jdubs premarket prep videos to get an idea of what to expect and what key levels to keep and eye on.  Trying to a Long position on a stock that is downtrending in the HTF is pretty risky.  Also, something new that I'm just learning is to use the QQQ and SPY price action to determine relative strength and weakness for the stock I'm considering to trade.  In a nut shell, the break and retest strategy works best when you pair it up with multiple confluences like HTF thesis, current market direction, and relative strength/weakness.  I'm very new to trading, but what' I'm learning so far is that simply applying the break and retest to a key level isn't optimal.  We need to start line up the strategy with multiple confluences.

messages.author: Tito Frescado
messages.content:
Overview:
FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. I thought that since AMZN was above YDH so early in the day with a strong uptrend behind it on all timeframes that it might continue. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill. Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 

Setup Details:
Entry: FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. AMZN showed a strong uptrend on all timeframes. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill.
Position Size: Discuss the position size taken and why. Mention any adjustments made for risk management.
Exit: Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 
Profit/Loss: Lost 20 dollars on the trade. On second look, the was perhaps too optimistic trade on FMOC day when you expect the trade to stay within the PDR. Perhaps my entry was too aggressive because although the hammer was red and might have worked on another day, it was FMOC day. I should have waited for a green confirmation candle close above the red hammer that I entered on. It would have kept me out of the trade.

Rating: ⭐ [5 of Stars]
⭐ 1 Star: Although the setup was good. I should have waited for more confirmation that it wanted to continue above YDR.
Key Takeaways:
Lesson 1: Expect price to stay within YDR on FOMC day before 2:00. Wait for price to reach YDH or YDL looking for a quick scalp in the opposite direction
Lesson 2: Look for solid confirmation before entering a trade on FMOC day.
Missed Opportunities: No. the was a retest of the top of the daily gap but not enough room to move before hitting resistance at YDH.
Other Factors: FOMC

messages.author: thamerb777
messages.content:
Thanks.
-Tuesday: Neutral. No direct impact from the Richmond Index, but it might move with QQQ. Hold off on trades until closer to earnings on Thursday.
-Wednesday: Markets may start positioning for Thursday’s earnings. If PMIs are strong and Waller is dovish, consider a small long position (calls) ahead of earnings. If data is weak, stay neutral or hedge (puts).
-Thursday: Avoid trading during the day unless economic data significantly impacts tech sentiment (e.g., strong Durable Goods Orders might support a pre-earnings rally). Post-earnings, if GOOGL beats (EPS > $2.01) and provides strong AI/Cloud guidance, go long for Friday. If it misses, short the initial drop or buy a dip if the long-term outlook remains solid.
-Friday: Post-earnings reaction dominates. If GOOGL beat expectations, expect a gap up—ride the momentum with a long position. If it missed, short the drop or buy a dip if the outlook is strong. Strong Consumer Sentiment could further support GOOGL.

messages.author: 305 Trader
messages.content: HI. I reviewed this set up just now. This set up served very well the 1st 15 min candle ORB strategy but to the d/s.  It did retest even twice the low. Now, only thing I can say against that CALL was that on the Daily chart the stock was overextended in relationship to the ADR of the previous 9 days. Context is so important. The stock opened up following a huuge gap, so I would have wanted to wait and observe what direction it would pick. There is this rule that I try reminding myself that says: Do not buy stock that is overextended to either side.  Oscar, buy CALLS in discount area and go short in Premium area. This simple yet tried and true notion can save you from FOMO and from doing a bad entry. Everything is possible, yes, but the odds are not in our favor when we buy when the stock or index is overextended.

messages.author: Kam
messages.content:
Took a pretty lousy trade on PLTR this morning and wanted to share my review. Open to any thoughts or other feedback! 

PLTR – 6/26/25
Setup: BnR 1m OB Continuation + 5m OR Retest
Result:  Loss (–1R)
Confidence on Entry: 3/5
Sizing: Base Size

What Happened:
Strong opening drive in PLTR with RS and news catalyst. Took an anticipatory reclaim entry off the 1m OB after visual strength near prior pivot, but skipped confirmation and entered on candle stretch. Market context was improving, 5m candle looked like a nice hammer, but pullback was steep and PLTR can be trappy. Trade hit stop shortly after entry. Earlier missed trade off PMH may have been a factor as well as pass on NVDA OB play prior day.

Classification:
False Form – Anticipatory Entry 
Good structure idea, poor entry execution. Trade plan shifted from OB pullback to reclaim without re-evaluation.

Primary Mistake:
Premature Entry – No Reclaim Confirmation

What Worked:
- Avoided forced dip buys in semis
- Honored stop and respected system
- Solid structure read on RS and catalyst

Needs Work:
– Entry discipline on reclaims
– Slight emotional residue from passing on NVDA prior day and PMH retest at the open
– OB sequence clarity and reclaim filters need reinforcement

messages.author: Kam
messages.content:
Hey Jatin - this is a tricky one for me since it's lower timeframe bearish, but HTF bullish. I didn't take this trade, but here's how I hypothetically would have managed it:

The 155 area was the reclaim level after the 5m hammer into the PDH level so once we get a candle close above that level I'd draw out the full OB and use that as structural invalidation. I'd have taken profit like you at HOD. That nasty wick held that OB - and after taking profits at HOD, the trade is de-risked so if it comes back it comes back, and stop out for BE or small loss depending on your size. I probably would have sold into new high pops at 156.5, but these setups usually work fast and hit PMH quickly. So once it stalled out, I probably would have been cautious when got back under the HOD pivot and 1m/5m OB to stop out of trailers. 

There's different ways to trail though - I tend to use OB's and structure to trail, but others like <@1121621751764369501> use trendlines and EMAs. <@1014389514141184074> uses a 5m timer! Just have to find your style!

Easy to play armchair QB with hindsight bias i know 😂  But that's my 2c, thanks for asking - this was a good thought exercise!

I know Tony & Jack took this trade cos they were just talking about it in Traders Lab - so definitely check out Tony's trade review of this tonight!

messages.author: 305 Trader
messages.content:
hi Francine. I thought of making a review to your trade, because I found it pretty good a teaching for me. You got the direction right, as you can see Apple had been on a upward ride for days. So, yes momentum had been there, thats 1. Now as for your entry, since AAPL demonstrated that the uptrend was clear for several days, that bounce from your wedge in purple lines was your POI and not the ORB you drew. Additionally, the break out from that wedge had confluence with one of your KL. This was a powerful event you should have not missed.  If I was to take a swing or even a day trade on AAPL, it would have been on the break and retest (in a smaller tf) of that confluence. In the higher timeframe (swing) this was also, the continuation signal of the market in its uptrend. So, your entry was late when you decided to make it off of that ORB way above. 
Or, if taking the swing off of that ORB, it would need to be after the mitigation with a strong rejection to that POI or ORB you drew. I think you took it on the 1st impulse instead  and hence you had the expected pullback. I am curious how the symbol did on Friday since I had not traded since last Tuesday.

messages.author: EeveeWinter
messages.content: You were in a 332.5C, with the underlying moving to 335.10. But for most of the trade: You were initially OTM — The move took time — Delta was likely sub-0.50 for a good chunk of the time. Since delta starts low and only ramps up as price nears the strike, your option didn’t gain value linearly. Early in the move, delta was low; as the move slowed, delta rose — but then theta canceled the gain.

messages.author: Royal191
messages.content: Hi Anne, glad to see you are able to identify the issue so quickly. Now you can make a plan/rules to avoid this happening in the future. Revenge trading is one of those things that will wreck your mental capital and your actual capital. Especially revenge trading with names like TSLA or META. If you take a bad trade, try to step away from the screens for a few mins and clear your head. If you decide to come back and look for another trade you have to treat the next trade as its own trade and trade it without the emotions from the prev trades. Once you have the prev loss in your mind it will be easier to make mistakes like taking profits too early or even holding too long to make up the loss and letting a green trade turn red.

messages.author: 305 Trader
messages.content:
Hi Max. Thanks for sharing your trades which lost, I think is brave of you to decide to do self accountabilty on your losses and to share. We learn a lot from our losses and other people’s.,I took the time to draw some thoughts and took screenshots of your charts. Then I draw where I would enter, not knowing anything about HTF and a narrative on those tickers, just purely intraday patterns from the chart.  I trade patterns. 

I know it may be a different style for you. I think the market always form patterns like a gradual ascending (long) staircase, or a gradual descending (short) staircase. These patterns repeat over and over intraday but it needs time to form. 

Trading the 1st 15 mins of market open in my opinion requires a very strong HTF narrative formed, a strong understanding of the stock and a good gut for being ok with accepting larger losses.  And this is because the market has not formed patterns yet, its only reaction to pre market and gaps. 
I trade differently. This is what I watch for, I follow a staircase formation which breaks price structure along  key levels of confluence and only act when a key level is respected as support or resistance to go in continuation. I think Neto trades this way more often than the other coaches. Its a matter of choice and backtesting. Very important this b/t part. 

Lastly, one advice, do not do initial mental stop loss. This is only for traders who have an extraordinary emotional management achieved and a rigurous discipline aka Jack or Tony.  I am not exaggerating. Our job is to defend our profits. The market’s is to provide the wins. 

Here is my screenshots of where I would have liked to enter safely.

messages.author: jav
messages.content: Hey Max, I am certainly not someone to be giving advice but somethings I have learned and advice I have been given that you may find useful. In regards to a mental stop or a hard stop, with a hard stop if the candle hits your stop area your going to be stopped out of the trade, which is why Tony and Jack always say to wait for candle closure. I used to always use a hard stop but I would mostly get stopped out and then the stock would take off in the way I wanted it to, which can be very nerve racking. I am not sure what your set up was on those 2 trades but Tony advises you to stay with one set up when you are a new trader, whether that be the 5 minute opening range, premarket or previous day and just stay with whichever your most comfortable with. I personally only trade the 5 minute opening range break. I also switched to a 5 minute chart because trading off a 1 minute chart I found to be super stressful. The only con to a 5 minute chart when waiting for candle closure is if you get a big candle the opposite of your trade your stop is going to be much lower than you wanted it to be, but that has only happened once in TSLA.  I hope you found something useful in this and good luck in your trading journey.

messages.author: 305 Trader
messages.content: Setting up the correct stop loss (where the trade invalidates) at start is a real art guys and sticking to it with a mechanical stop loss is the only way to create a good habit of accepting the risk of trading. No hope, just a mechanical trade plan implemented with discipline. Mental stop losses do not help us create good habits. They are helpful when the disciplined trader has become the operator. Before this, mental stop losses are a demon for me.

messages.author: Kam
messages.content:
No worries, glad it helped. Love that attitude, keep it up! 

Definitely backtest the 5m closures so that you can find what works for your style/system. 

Waiting for 5m closes can widen the stops sometimes and kill the R/R so stop placement is pretty critical and keep time of day top of mind. 

For stops, focus on the level that's being reclaimed (if trading a reclaim). I used to be really bad  at reclaims when I first joined this room (ask <@1062934985109160057> 😂), but now they're becoming one of my best setups so definitely keep practicing! 

Generally from my data it's ok for a level to be retested after a reclaim, but these setups should move pretty quickly so don't want to give too much room.

messages.author: Das Wookie
messages.content:
I broke a number of my rules today with my trades... not all of my rules, but quite a bunch of them:
- Don't trade after 11 - I couldn't resist the afternoon downturn to go short on PLTR and GOOG
- Don't revenge trade - I lost my first entry trying a trade with RGTI, so I exited the position, played a Uno Reverse card on my position and immediately re-entered, and lost again! -$4k on both. Doggie! That sucked!
- Don't chase! DON'T F-ING CHASE! Got it! - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't force trades - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Sit on your hands and WAIT for your entry - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't flit from one trade to another - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Max of 3 daily options trades... I entered in on 11 trades in total today
- Don't over trade - See max 3 trades
- If I do well on either of my initial 2 trades, and I'm green for the day... you're done! - I only had the two trades on TSLA and INTC, but I got greedy and wanted more. It worked out, but it shouldn't have. I got lucky that it worked out, but I SHOULD have just taken the initial win and shut down for the day and protected my wins. With the RGTI trade, I nearly went negative on all of my initial TSLA trade gains
- Capture gains at key levels and level out of position! - I ended up losing a potential $33k because I didn't take profit at a key level
- Don't move your stop! didn't exit at my initial stop, and then moved my stop, twice, trying to recapture the potential unrealized gain instead of just capturing the trade where my initial stop was set.

messages.author: Das Wookie
messages.content:
I generally am not, no. Sometimes I'll have it on in the background, just so I can listen in on what the boys are doing, but for the most part I'm doing my own thing. I trade more than B&R, and watch more symbols... so if the Mag 7 aren't doing anything interesting, and my thesis for the day ended up wrong... well then I'll just go wherever my watchlist leads me... and where one of my setups appears.

Today, it would have been VERY difficult to have not printed if you were still paying attention after 11, and the bottom of the bucket fell out for the next 2 hours. Today was an amazing velocity trade to the downside!!! This sorta day doesn't come around very often... at least not without some tweet from the pumpkin king that causes the entire market to panic. I was in a $40k trade last time that happened, and my jaw hit the floor!!! "Holy ****!!!! What the... What just happened!??? Babe! Pull up the news. Did the pentagon just explode or a dirty bomb go off in NY or LA????" as I watched TRILLIONS of dollars vaporize before our very eyes. I didn't have the emotional stability to cut my loss RIGHT THEN and switch to PUTS. I was too locked in on my CALL trade, and didn't want to cut the position... thinking "This HAS to come back! This is insane! What is the market doing?!!! C'Mon!!! Come back up! Please! PLEASE!!!" where as now, I'd just cut it. RIGHT then. Without Mercy! "It's just numbers." and these days, I'd just SCRAM the trade. That's also the basis for some of my rules. We all establish our own rules. Sometimes I break mine, but I do my level best to only do it for a VERY good reason. Sometimes tho, I slip up... like with my two RGTI trades today which was the basis of violating more than half of the rules I broke today. Of course, had I NOT broken that rule, and tried to chase a win there... I'd have already been out of the market and entirely missed this afternoons drop because I'd have been out of the market already and on to do other things.

messages.author: Das Wookie
messages.content: In the interest of sharing, I had an absolutely HORRIFIC trading day today. I lost my first two trades for the day, and should have followed my rule and walked away right then. Instead, I chased, and entered a trade without a proper setup entry JUST because it looked like it might be good. I sized WAY up to 400 contracts too try and make up for my loss. Turns out tho, there were NO options traders on that symbol. The Bid/Ask Spread was MASSIVE with a $3.50 spread, but I didn't notice until I went to exit. There were NO buyers. NONE!!! I pulled up the Time & Sales, and I thought the data connection was broken. I pulled up history, and I saw some trades of mostly 1 contact, a few for 2 contracts, a rare 5 or 10, and then my 400 contacts buy. There we no other options trades. I couldn't exit! Well I could, but with THAT spread, it was BRUTAL. I listed the limit sale at the ASK, and nothing happened for 30 seconds. I dropped my price by $0.10. Then again. And again. And again... and And AND!!! I finally sold my position after dropping my ASK $3.40 when it FINALLY sold. I went from being up and being green for the day, and turning that into a loss of $140k. I was DEVASTATED. I couldn't believe I got myself into that position. I found a stock that was moving, and did well... and destroyed myself. I did a few trades after that, but there was ZERO chance of being green. I was just TRYING to limit the damage. I ended up -$137k for the day. It's not the end of the world for me. I'm still green for the week, but it's embarrassing, emotional, and gravely stupid you have done. But it is what it is! So... There's my last 6 days of trading. It seems like people only ever post their gains or complain about losses. I traded emotionally. I chased. I kept increasing size to try and get back to even. I did ask if this on a 0DTE Friday of all days!!! I was, an idiot! So, I'm falling on my sword here.

messages.author: DWalt Trades
messages.content: Running hard stops is completely fine just always be sure to be aware of what the other time frames are shaping up like compared to the one you are trading on, and have a proper and defined risk. Jack and Tony emphasize the importance of a candle close so eventually you will get the grasp after enough wick outs then price running straight to TP, you will be able to identify how to avoid it in the future. I have saved my myself from fake invalidations and had very good trades play out by not letting a wick stop me, but I’ve also had some trades seriously run away by waiting for candle close. So It has its pros and cons and eventually, based on your own trading style, you will find out what works best for you and a lot of times it is based on situational context. But in the meantime I do strongly recommend a proper and defined risk level if you are running hard stops. I used to trade the 1M as well, but now I only enter and scale on it. My risk and target is always managed and defined on the 5 and 15 and its taken a lot of the unnecessary losses out, stopped me from entering fakeout/sweeps, and improved my win rate and trading overall.

messages.author: DWalt Trades
messages.content: I recommend doing trade review videos on all your trades, bar by bar in replay mode, considering more than one time frame, everyday after session. Along with mass backtesting sessions that are recorded. This will help you identify how and where to cut down the overtrading and hold your trades until they either are invalidated or objective filled. I say this because technically your first entry @ 25,798.50 was never invalidated, and your initial trade could’ve been held to main objectives instead of having multiple smaller trades inside of the initial main position. This could have been a one and done day. I used to find myself in trade reviews saying “I should have just held my initial entry” on a breakeven or small red day that could’ve very well been green days and ultimately those deep reviews are where my improvement came from. That is the main reason I responded to your first text in here about it because I immediately identified something that I used to fall victim to as well on your chart so any questions just tag me here and I am happy to help!

I didn't make the dollar gains but I made some mental gains 💪 

p.s. I only entered their live trades because I see what Jack and Tony see and have the same thesis. No entering in blindly.

messages.author: John H
messages.content:
Quick post here regarding my oversized losses from Friday's session. I decided I would play WDC, and as we witnessed memory names had a brutal selloff. But more importantly what did I do wrong. I came in simply unprepared. WDC is not a ticker I normally trade. I had zero premarket prep. No key levels to watch and no exit plan when the trade turned against me. I failed to check a higher time frame to see that we were selling off. I should have marked off the open high low on the 5 min. candle as is often discussed here and I didn't do that either. So, an entire solid month of very good trading cost me more than 1/2 my gains for January. 

To make matters worse, I continued to trade. While I won most of those trades, I took yet another big loss on SNDK. I entered very late chasing more downside and the stock reversed near end of day. I'm saying this as a reminder to myself and for others that might be newer traders how very important it is to map out your game plan. To stay disciplined. To know your exit point if the trade turns against you. To not overtrade by taking too many trades, or revenge trade to try and recoup losses. Had I followed <@1014389514141184074> Friday advice I would of had a much better week & month. I was up $42K for January, I ended with $16K. I was disciplined all month long until Friday. That is truly how quickly you can make careless errors and give it all back. 

I still have a very high win rate, but you'll find that does not matter if you let trades get worse by the minute and don't get out when they are clearly not working. I will come to the market prepared, or I will not trade. That is something I have to follow for myself. We ride again Monday, and you all will see me turn this around. Best of luck everyone.

messages.author: Fred
messages.content:
Hi, here my trade for today. The result wasn't very good : two losses (-2.7R) and one win (+2R).
What's your opinion on the process ?

In 1h TF, we broke and retested the previous high from last Wednesday. Last Thursday, we broke the Wednesday low, which we then retested on Friday. The plan today was either a continuation of the bearish trend (break of the red zone/PDL) or a rejection of the previous low/PDH (red zone).

In 15m TF I identified 2 potential areas for a reverse (green area, which coincides with the red zone for rejection in 1h TF) and 1 area for continuation (red area).

On the 1m TF, the scenario is bullish; we disregard the bearish continuation opportunity and focus on the reversal. Based on my key zones identified on the 1-hour and 15-minute charts, I took puts on breakouts and retested previous 1-minute highs (market structure/yellow area).

messages.author: Joebag009
messages.content: I had a big AHA that if it was my cash account I never would have risked subsequent NVDA trades, so I think I might be trading my cash account with 720 in it (and taking profit out each day) for a few days to build my psychology up with a fresh start. Another big aha is that with 0s and even generally the first trade is the best trade and unless I have a new thesis or proved continuation (preferably with divergence from the market), re-entering is a bad idea - looking at my trading notes I have had wins in the past on re-entering for continuation, especially in big dumps around key levels and in grinds but this week it has not worked out (as in net profit) and eaten my meagre profits. Instead of blaming the market, I am adding new habits of pausing between trades and to reload I will develop a quick checklist for myself - did it defend a key level and continue or is it just teasing me, or is it just teasing me as it mirrors qqq moves (hey, it got Jack with TSLA this week, I need to be kind to myself, but also prevent it next time), is it over-extended on the stock, and with qqq overall? What is the daily tf trend? Are Jack and Tony still in and either not at target or just tp1? Tp2 and “to the moon” are horrible places for re-entry, even if it is holding a key level or 1 minute timeframe. Reminder to myself that re-entries pay less, fail more often, and lose money more quickly than initial setups.

Elite athletes don’t rise to the occasion; they fall to their level of training. -Neto Moreno

Theme of the day and Aha #1: SLOW THE HELL DOWN, be patient, DO NOT LONG OR SHORT IN CHOP, AT EDGE OF RANGES, AT HIGH OR LOW OF DAY… so in other words looking at a 1/5/15 min TF of a daily chart, the move looks HUGE, it makes us GREEDY, it makes us FEEL LIKE A BAD TRADER for “only” capturing xyz… but really, “perfect” is timing to SAFELY capture a relatively TINY part of that move with HIGH expectancy and CONSISTENCY. Doing it with 0s like JT (Jack and Tony) grants extra rewards.

messages.author: Joebag009
messages.content:
Struggle I identified last week was juggling too much at once: evals with MNQ and FZ (FlowZone), options with JT. It is too many accounts. My original thesis was options in the AM followed by MNQ all day, it is 24/5 after all. For almost a month now I tried trading globex, I tried Asia and London, I tried open (5 central, Sunday afternoon etc.) and my results are mixed – very much like my early trading journey with medium wins and big losses wiping it all out, plus mental exhaustion, replicated in the youtubers I watch who trade all these sessions. One clean setup over and over, like Neto talks about often, and JT implicitly in their Youtube vids. Overtrading for me boils down to: desperation, greed, fear, poor impulse control, and revenge trading. I have conquered them individually, but need to be ready for any to pop up randomly depending on my emotional state and how the day is going. 

Today I did okay with options and then shifted my focus to futures which prevented overtrading and losses by maintaining focus on one thing at a time, however now I’m worried I won’t pass evals without sustained focus on a big move. I will need to be disciplined if trades don’t line up to protect gains, let some evals fail, and perhaps focus a morning just on futures – there is more of a grind in props but much lower cash risk compared to options with my own money – one con of options is the same as like 3 evals or more if they are on sale, however it is a journey to get to profitability with props.

messages.author: Joebag009
messages.content:
Big aha with FZ today is that just like with JT I overtraded and didn’t quite match his execution. My goal today was to trade less and more focused on his stops and setups. While I missed some of the moves and botched the execution a bit, I at least paid attention to him with a new type of mindset and followed through and am up in 1 out of two Apex accounts. I could feel my frontal cortex straining, doing REPS, as I breathed through my emotions and focused on market structure. I stayed OUT of MULTIPLE trades, that WOULD NOT HAVE WORKED. So that was a huge psych win for me today, which I need cause I feel like I’m on the cusp of either making it big or completely losing my mind. FZ did 3 trades today, I did more with less size, same pattern as with JT and re-entries, but a bit easier managed on the charts so less friction. 

12:30 PM Eastern behaviour of futures seems to change a bit and most consistent pros are done here if not a lot sooner. Big AHA: a price move on a chart is not equal, cons and futures move different depending on price volume and market structure. “d’uh” but seeing it in real time is different than seeing it in a lesson. A “choppy/grindy” move in both futures and contracts is a lot crappier than a clean smaller move with strong volume and direction. Again “d’uh”, but starting to see it is making a big difference in my emotions and execution. Also after 12:30 fills get worse on stopping out and taking profits making it harder to stack up wins that recover from even small losses (because bad fills make them even 3x worse than AM exit with the same distance stop). In other words, what the chart shows is a lie, the risks of extracting money from what you see go UP.

messages.author: Huy
messages.content:
first candle rule (5 mins opening orb range) is something that is always good to map out and see so you can build some context. If the price is below both the PDL and PML, that would be one confluence for me. I like to take key levels into consideration such as the HTF levels. I am going to steal Jack's words but WMT offered that beautiful pop and fade today. 
PDL + 128.10 key support would have been a low risk entry. PML and PMH should be used with caution though since the levels created in this session does not have a lot of volume so its a thing we can look at if there's a HTF thesis and PM levels is the only thing that we can work off in a *trending* day.

if presented with this trade, I would only take a short off the 128.10 level that I mentioned either or go long if it can break and retest above 129.70 since this would violate the current structure and can shift for the upside. 

p.s. if you trade with wmt more in the future, you can watch the XLP index for another confluence

The 2nd and 3rd pulls look weaker to me because momentum hadn’t really established yet - GOOG was consolidating and the market was choppy. Retests tend to work best after clear displacement & quick pullbacks

Just because the 9:42 entry didn't go up & have continuation doesn't mean it was a bad trade according to the system. The 2nd & 3rd entries look good because they went up, but for every chart that looks like this, there's one that fails to the downside also

messages.author: JP400
messages.content: Good morning. The black line is the 8 EMA based on the Chart TF (so 1min in this case). I use this in conjunction with the pink line which is the 3 EMA also based on the Chart TF (so 1 min in this case). I want the 3 to be above the 8. If I notice the 3 and 8 constantly crossing, I am hesitant. If the 3 holds above, then I feel more confident that the price will hold in the direction I intend. The orange dotted line is the VWAP, and I want price to be on my side of it. The blue solid line is the 3 EMA based on a 15min TF. In theory it reminds me that the 15m is also holding my direction (as long as I pay attention to it). But, as with anything... Sometimes my eyes completely ignore them when desperation sets in. Call it blind panic.

messages.author: Kam
messages.content: Hey there, this looks like a good trade to me. If you mark out the entire 9:40 1 candle, you'll see that price never closed below that range + the 211 key level. I'd say you may want to increase stop width on this one so that your stop is more inline with structure. May need to take lower size to account for the wider risk. Nice trade idea tho!

messages.author: Kam
messages.content:
I think the key is to really switch the focus to the logic or 'why' behind the level. The reason we trade BnR and key levels is because we are looking for evidence of prior resistance turning to support and vice versa. The lower wick on the 9:56 candle shows that buyers are stepping in to defend 211 - a key level both intraday as well as last week's high. There were multiple confluences here. 3c close below such a key level on a $200 underlying price wouldn't be enough evidence to stop out for me personally. But if your data & backtested rules say to stop out, then definitely stop out!  

That being said - it's really easy to use hindsight bias on this one as the trade worked. If you were in the trade from <@722212337460838421> entry and holding as that lower wick forms, likely you're going to want to stop out at lows as it pushes below your stop. The confidence to place stops consistently and execute usually comes from really understanding & internalizing the logic behind the level and of course there's no substitute for a lot of reps where you practice doing the same thing repeatedly. I'd really suggest checking out Jack's video on accepting risk - it's a really good one and worth reviewing https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1222375088318447637/1467221561898569911

messages.author: Martin_AT
messages.content:
One day later I looked at the trade again and I found out the mistake I made and why it confused me. Simply the entry was too late. I make this mistake from time to time and this usually leads to a bad RWR. So when something like yesterday happens my SL gets triggered and then price moves to TP.
So the logical "solution" for me is to really focus on entries very close to the level and if the trade is already gone not tho chase the trade anymore.
In the screenshot you can see that if you just move the Long-Tool a bit lower then the trade would have worked out. So simple in hindsight but sometimes very hard to understand during the "hectic" trading session. Therefore will adapt my "daily remainder" so that I can avoid this in the future.
Maybe you had the same issue and this helps you as well

messages.author: Rodo
messages.content: Honestly at this stage, just use market orders. You’re still learning, so speed and execution matter more than getting the perfect fill. Right now the bigger issue is hesitation and missing entries—not a few cents on price.Once you get comfortable with that and everything starts to feel more natural, then you can build on it step by step—and eventually switch over to limit orders as you refine your execution. Also, it depends on your size. If you’re trading a small number of contracts, market orders won’t hurt you much. But as you start trading more contracts, getting filled efficiently matters a lot more—so that’s when limit orders become more important.

messages.author: Jatin
messages.content:
You have already answered your questions. The contracts on NVDA move a lot less if you compare to AMD contracts. Just an example, if you are trading 4 cons in AMD, the usual risk is around $60 per contract. Say you lost that trade and on 4 contracts it will be -$240 on that trade.

Then you trade 4 contracts on NVDA, with usual risk being $30 per contract. Say you won this one and took a full exit at 2RR. Now you won and profit comes to $240 for your 4 contracts.

See now you are break even even after you took 2RR winner. The issue is sizing. On the first trade you were risking $240 but on second one you were risking $120.
To match the risk in the trades, you would have traded 8 contracts on NVDA with risking same $240 on the trade, now if you won at 2RR, you would have profitted $480

Let me know if this makes sense

messages.author: 305 Trader
messages.content: The higher prob set up for an entry, is not just any type of B&R, but rather, the B&R and continuation of the top of a price structure formed (ceiling), or the inverse, the B&R and continuation  of the bottom of the structure formed (floor). When you wait for this to happen you will win most of the time. Thus, the best entry in your screenshot was later when the $400 level was broken up, retested and continuation. Do this, over and over, repeat.

messages.author: Kam
messages.content:
Hey there, 

The fact that you can clearly identify the actual behaviors so specifically is probably more important long term than the $1k itself.

For me personally, the biggest reset after rough patches has always been reducing the pressure to “make it back” and just getting laser focused on execution - especially if I know execution was the issue (vs. market or normal variance). I usually size down until I see that I'm trading my process / setups consistently again. 

Regarding drills - I think this depends on each person to a certain extent, but something that helped reduce my trade count was really focusing on my filters for (dis)qualifying a setup. If you try and actively ask yourself 'why not' when considering a trade it can help with awareness & logic vs just getting locked in on the patterns we've been trained to recognize in the charts. 

Hope that helps - love the attitude 💪

messages.author: nasman
messages.content:
That "why not" filter makes perfect sense. I definitely get locked into just seeing the pattern and ignoring the messy context around it. I'm going to implement that exact defensive mindset to help disqualify the sloppy setups.

To build on that and really strengthen my ability to identify high probability trades, what does your personal in the moment checklist look like? Is there a specific sequence of things you check off before you finally decide to pull the trigger?

I’ve been trying to discipline myself to look for multiple confluences, like checking key levels (PDH/PDL or a 5ORB) along with VWAP, a 9 EMA crossover, and indices support (SPY/QQQ) to make sure the market is behind me. But sometimes it feels like a lot to process simultaneously when the market opens.

messages.author: Kam
messages.content:
Awesome, I'm glad that helped! I would honestly try to keep things as simple as possible - when you backtest or review your trades, try and really break down the logic of the trade. What makes your best trades 'good', and vice versa for your worst trades. 

For me my checklist boils down to a few categories: location, context, setup quality & trigger.

But to your point, that can be a lot to process in the heat of the decision moment, especially on a 1m chart. It takes time and a lot of practice, but I would definitely start with your data and try figure out where your strengths & weaknesses are. 

Confluences can definitely add to the probabilities, but I'd say even this depends on your specific system.

messages.author: 305 Trader
messages.content: Hey JP, I dont know if you are a woman or a man, i just wanted to tell you that I felt both laugh and compassion with your message. Laugh because you said “we throw the brain out of the window”. As for the rest, sounds like you have tried many things but the one thing still eludes you is the adhering to simple rules after observing what works. The only answer is: keep trying to figure it out for your self. But, with only 1x con. No more. Forget about the notion of getting rich or making a living out of this. Just sit down and trade with just 1x con, adjust your entries. The market has turned difficult for the day trader (not the swing trader with capital to hold). Corrections are very brief, ranges are long for some symbols. so go long for the most part. I also had a bad probably worst month of the year in May. Also, because I departed from what made me profitable in 2026 to try a new strategy which proved it is not working in this market with the Elliott waves I was trying. So, what we have to do is to adhere to a set of 3-4 rules and pick up our brains from the garbage can and force it to sit, “just to sit”and to wait for the rules to present themselves in the form of a set up and hit it. But also to act early as soon as they are spotted, this is the flow, to be able to cruise in low speed for a while but to switch gear quickly when the rules present themselves or viceversa. There it lies the fluidity of the same day trader.

messages.author: Royal191
messages.content:
Guys, it’s a learning process. I am in the same boat as some of you where the first modules are pretty slow. At first I thought about mentioning it too, but then I watched the recap videos and the live sessions ( even though I was present for the live sessions). These videos are sometimes 2 hours long and provide a ton of value. Seeing the thought process and reactions to certain things as they happen will not be seen in the modules. I am happy that Tony and Jdub are compromising and will now be releasing two modules per week; this shows us that we are in the right place and they are here to help us.

For the members that are saying it’s not fair for the month to month payers. Think about it this way, would it be fair for a month to month payer to access the modules they are looking for and drop off after one month because they learned everything they wanted? Now that wouldn’t be fair to the members that paid for the annual or the life time, would it? Tony said the reason for release timeframe is not a retention grabber since most of the members are either annual or lifetime, but think about this: Would anyone be willing to put countless hours and money into a program and sell it for $200? Because that’s essentially what happens when someone sign up for one month and access the modules they want then cancel, while others paid $1,000 to $2,000. The free content they provide on YouTube is worth more than $200 per month in my opinion, but that’s just me. Btw, I am one of the month to month payers myself, but that is because I am waiting to join the mastermind. We are all here to learn, trust the process. The other stuff will get worked out in time, it’s a new program...

messages.author: Brian
messages.content: Don't go on tilt. Slow and steady wins the game. Put it behind you and take good trades to build it back up slowly. Every time I try to make a "get it back" trade it fails 100% of the time.

messages.author: BFX
messages.content: Don’t try to get it back asap because you will continue to fail, give a break and study your mistakes, then correct it with potential good trades, you will surely cover it in due time and learn how to cut your loses

messages.author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
messages.content: That's a hard situation. If I were you, I would spend some time working on psychology, without trying to get the money back. Just strict risk management with small amounts of risk. And then trade with decent risk, because with a good RR and win rate you will get the money back in a short period of time, but only after you stop losing money doing things you weren't planning to do. First clear your mind, then back to trade.

messages.author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
messages.content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY **

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

If you’re green for the week, only trade with a small **PORTION** of your profits. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

If you’re red for the week, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

Please confirm you have read this with a 👍🏼

messages.author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
messages.content:
Hi guys, this might be interesting to anyone struggling with RR like I do. I revised my trades (made during live sessions) of the last month, and here's the stats.

There were 10 losing trades (exit on SL, actually 9, but for one trade I couldn't remember the setup so I counted it as a loss in terms of this analysis), and 12 winning trades. 3 of the losses were on FOMC day, so they can actually be excluded as I shouldn't have taken them at all. But still.

RR of the winning trades: 
9,39
4,73
4,07
2,08
3,29
2,05
4,32
3,04
1,4
3,16
5,18
3,3
That's if I didn't close position until the initial stop loss was hit and just waited until the trend changes or the day ends, and took profit at maximum (which you never know for sure, of course).

So there was only one trade with RR less than 2.
I'm actually red this month due to uneven risk management and selling 30-50% of the position at HoD most of the times (I trade 4-6 cons).

So I made these calculations to understand what kind of result I would have now if I only sold at 2RR or 3RR.
Check the screenshot, it's all there. 
If you trade a small number of cons as I do you might come to the same conclusion as I did, that even closing the whole position at 3RR without taking partial scales at HoD/LoD might have been rather profitable.

Of course, that's a random month and these stats aren't really representative. But I personally want to try something like selling 4 cons out of 6 at 2RR, and the rest (2) at 3RR.

Hope this is helpful 🙌

messages.author: Jdub
messages.content:
Just caught back up with the chat.

I'd like to give my thought on some of the things said.

Taking signals is not a long term solution. I can promise you that if you solely rely on signals that you will lose in the long term and let me break it down. A great example of this is yesterday and today. Let's say you took the NVDA trade towards the downside yesterday. Most likely you would have sold on the first push towards the downside because you didn't understand the overall context of the trade. This was a higher timeframe thesis laid out in the pre market prep and we talked about this over the weekend as well. If the market is weak we will be look for NVDA towards the downside. And if you didn't understand this, you would have no conviction to hold for the move towards the downside even though there was a very good chance that it would head lower. 

And today for example lets say you missed the trade yesterday and now you got into NVDA this morning. I mentioned that its a completely different setup and that you have to keep a tight stop on this one as its more aggressive since its close to open. Once again if you didn't understand this it would be hard to capture. Price ended up rejecting off PML and reversing. When it came back up I stopped out quickly because I knew that price was most likely going to reverse. 

What I'm trying to say is that the problem with taking signals is that when you lose you end up taking a full R loss and sometimes even more since you will hold past your stop and when your in a winning trade you sell as soon as your in the green. You can't hold because you don't have conviction for yourself since your relying on someone else but yourself.

And that's just the reality of taking signals. That's why we teach to fish for yourselves. You think Neto became profitable by taking signals. He took some of the concepts that we teach and implemented it to his own trading, where he has his own system, strategy and edge in the markets. 

You saw my statistics for the month of August. Last two weeks of the month weren't the greatest but I ended up making over 29K with a 52% win rate because I had a 1:3.85 RR. My biggest loss was no more than 2K on a single day. When I lose I cut them quick and when I have an A+ setup I win big. 

I think most people understand this that have been in the room for a while but there's always a bunch that don't seem to understand this.

messages.author: demchy19
messages.content: Based on how the market moved and the pre-market setup, I was looking to trade scalps, focusing on retests and then targeting the PDH. For NVDA, my strategy was to enter after it broke out of the PMH. On the 1-minute chart, it showed a good displacement and then retested the PMH, where I entered with 6 contracts. I sold 3 contracts at the HOD, 2 contracts at halfway to the PDH, and the last contract once it broke below the PHOD. I didn't expect it to move too far but was aiming for just the PDH. But after it broke the PDH somehow was really weak compare to overall market (QQQ). I don't understand why. But I still saw a good trade.  Even with the AMZN trade, it was an intraday trade, and it didn’t have higher time frame thesis. The final target was the PDH. This is how I understand the situation and Jack’s approach.

messages.author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
messages.content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY**

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

**If you’re green for the week**, only trade with a small **PORTION of your profits**. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you’re red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, **Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you**. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

@everyone <@&1219020123218645102> 
Please confirm you have read this with a 🤑

messages.author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
messages.content:
Hey @everyone,

Just a quick heads-up on why trading options on FOMC days isn’t recommended. The market tends to be super volatile, with quick and unpredictable swings that can throw off even the best setups. Options can get especially tricky because premiums can spike or drop suddenly, making it hard to manage risk. It’s often better to wait until the market settles after the announcement, when you can trade with more clarity and less stress. 

Play it safe—there will always be better, more predictable days ahead!

<@&1219020123218645102>

messages.author: MVP Freestyle
messages.content: Choose options that expire 3 months out and swing trade it for a one to a few days. Take a contract slightly out of the money and let that appreciate to target. When you see perfect setup within that position buy a weekly contract to add  leverage to position. Essentially you are using the 3 month contract as a placeholder to add additional leverage on a shorter weekly timeframe when conditions are a no brainer.

messages.author: chunky_beast
messages.content:
<@584423632802021376> I’ll keep this as simple as possible, but please what I’m about to say isn’t meant to be gospel. I don’t like leaving questions unanswered if I can help it. 

When you look at the upcoming earnings you obviously expect an uptick in options trading into that particular week volume wise but what will almost always increase is options holders/open interest (OI) into those options. Using AAPL for example we have a large disparity between Call holders vs Put holders for Nov 1 Weekly’s so with this we can estimate that, as of Friday, the market is currently expecting bullish move into earnings. Plus as you get from Earnings Whispers, on the wider options there was a large volume of Feb 21 265C bought on Friday indicating some bull expects a move towards the upside in the longer term worth putting up $12M. With all this you can infer the market a bullish outlook on AAPL. 

A different case is GOOGL where we can see, as of Friday, there isn’t really any high expectations for explosive short term moves based on next weeks options OI. 

There are other equities you can look at with extremely high bullish option sentiment like AMZN and AMD. 

However this is just a gauge, not a crystal ball. Do your own research and ensure that this correlates with your strategy and playbook. Also if you’re gonna trade shorter dated options into earnings this isn’t a swing where you may lose 60-70%. You can easily lose your equity so you have to be comfortable with a 100% risk to capital overnight.

messages.author: Jdub
messages.content:
Its not really about being upset because as a human you are going to have emotions. It's all about what you do with those emotions. Am I upset when I take a bigger loss than expected. Of course but its all about how you respond and what you do after the fact. 

Trading is all a game about probabilities. I had conviction coming into this morning on TSLA for a continuation higher and guess what it didn't work out. I wanted to only risk around $2000 on this trade and ended up losing over $4000. But what happened next, I didn't do anything irrational or went to do something dumb. What I did was wait for another opportunity to setup. 

We ended up getting in SPY/ES which was a solid setup and is still working out as I type this out. 

As for relying on trading as income I didn't do this for the longest time. When I started out and even when I was profitable I was still delivering packages on the side. I would next recommend someone to do this full time without some sort of income coming in. Having to "rely" on trading is going to make it very difficult no matter who you are. 

Hope that makes some more sense.

messages.author: Lauren (lakatrades)
messages.content:
Thank you so much for your kind words and for recognizing the progress I’ve made it truly means a lot. The turning point for me was realizing that trading is all about psychological thinking. I had to learn to trust my knowledge and analysis, stay focused, and, most importantly, put emotions aside. Scaling up became much easier once I disciplined myself to stick to my trading plan, even though the setups stayed the same.
I’ve failed many times in the past because I didn’t stick to my plan and let emotions take over. It wasn’t until I committed to following the plan and staying disciplined that things started to change. Your mention of the Plateau of Latent Potentialreally hit home it’s such a powerful concept. There were so many moments when I felt like all my hard work wasn’t paying off, but eventually, everything started to click. Breaking through that phase took persistence and mindset shifts, and it felt amazing.
And no, I wasn’t upset at all by your gesture it actually made me happy! It’s great to know someone else understands and celebrates these milestones with me. Thank you so much for your support and encouragement it truly means a lot. 

Trust the process:)

messages.author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
messages.content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY**

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

**If you’re green for the week**, only trade with a small **PORTION of your profits**. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you’re red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you__**. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

**__Post-Holiday Trading Rules—Keep it Simple!__**

Today’s a short day after Thanksgiving, so expect **low volume** and **aggressive time decay**. It’s the perfect day to follow Mentor <@717554586843611217>’s advice: **watch the market only**. Turn off your brokerage, keep your charts on, and observe.

Remember, patience and discipline are key—there’s no need to force trades on days like this. Protect your capital and enjoy the rest of the holiday weekend!

Cheers!

messages.author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
messages.content:
For this setup to work best, there are some essential components:

Break of the level – Price needs to decisively break above or below the identified support or resistance.
Displacement – There should be a noticeable and strong move away from the level after the break. This ensures momentum and liquidity have shifted.
Retest – Price then retraces to retest the broken level.
Reaction – Finally, observe how price reacts during the retest. Strong rejection or confirmation signals the continuation of the move, offering a potential entry point.

Waiting for all these steps helps filter out false breakouts and increases the probability of a successful trade.

messages.author: BrokeAintNoJoke
messages.content:
I took the put trade yesterday because of multiple confluences. Price had retraced to the 61% fib level which correlated closely to the KL from previous PDH (I read trading bible over last weekend in books channel), PA was showing wicks up for a few bars showing sellers keeping price from breaching, there was a perfect test of the 1min ORB low and I think at the same time Qs and Spy showed weakness. 
I did not expect it to go down that quickly and exited all cons in the same fashion missing out on the huge move downward. I failed to leave trailers and scale. Also exited completely because just minutes prior I was in a call that at peak was 3x what I closed scaling 25% then 75% at breakeven. One lesson is I need to remember to leave trailers. Missed the big move downward. could have been a $3k day instead of $550 if I left trailers. That said I am happy that I am starting to see profitable entries.

messages.author: StevenP
messages.content: the 119 is 1 otm since stock price is 118. 1 otm strikes are a good balance for day traders to take. usually they have a good delta and the cost is decent. so it makes for good profit compared to cost. if you look at 2 otm sometimes the volume and open interest paired with your thesis on thinking the stock will go to $120 from 118 can be more profitable with a strong move towards the strike price. its really all about your risk and thesis on where the stock will go

messages.author: Lauren (lakatrades)
messages.content:
Copy of the email which I rec'd from Tony - 

If you’re reading this email, you've already experienced our Accelerator and may have some idea about Traders Lab.

However, I want to take a step back and give you a clear overview of what we’ve built and who this is for.

​

The mission of Traders Lab is to help traders optimize their performance to build a profitable career through tailored education.

Anyone that’s been able to become a profitable trader for a consistent period, it took them at least a few years to get everything right.

Our goal is to cut down those years of trials and tribulations.

​

We spent the last year building Traders Lab to ensure it perfectly supports every trader’s journey, regardless of their experience level.

Whether you’re starting out or are somewhere in that messy middle phase of your trading this is for you.

​

Here’s a breakdown of the three main pillars of Traders Lab:

Curriculum: Our playbooks are the most in-depth trading resources we’ve ever created.
​
Each playbook is meticulously designed with step-by-step modules, detailed examples, and proven best practices to ensure you’re learning the right way from day one.
​
Tailored: We understand that every trader has unique goals, experience levels, and learning styles.
​
That’s why our platform adapts to your specific needs, providing personalized learning paths and targeted resources.
​
Community: The ecosystem is at the heart of what makes Traders Lab special.
​
We’ve created an environment where both beginners and experienced traders can network, share insights, and grow together.

​

And that’s just the tip of the iceberg…

There’s so much more inside Traders Lab that I can’t fit into one email but will share more over the next couple of weeks.

​

Now, anyone can just say all of the above, but actually putting it to work is what drives us.

And, I want to show you just one example of what Traders Lab can do for you.

Here’s one of our top members Paul, who has been able to transform almost every aspect in his trading since joining Traders Lab.

Take a look at his recent message inside our community:


​

We've kept Traders Lab discrete and only available to a limited number of people.

We haven't let anyone from the public join us - that's how much we're prioritizing quality in this program.

Since you've experienced our Accelerator I wanted to extend you the opportunity to potentially join Traders Lab if it's a good fit.

All you have to do is a book a call with our team, if Traders Lab is a fit for your needs we'll offer you the opportunity to join us.

​Click here to book your call. (Link: https://calendly.com/traders-lab/success?utm_medium=mail&month=2025-02)

messages.author: Luke
messages.content:
that’s the way to go bro. It took me months to figure out why I was losing so much money. I finally figured out it was actually 2 big reasons:
1. Changing my sizing every trade, and since I had a small account I would size up every day. For example if I had a 3k account and made 1k, I would size up 1k the next day, which was stupid so it’s good you’re not doing that lol.
2. the other reason was because I was chasing and not taking A+ setups and taking trades for the sake of taking them.

messages.author: 𝕁𝕒𝕪
messages.content:
Bro, you lost $30k over 5 years. That’s $6k a year. $500’a month. $125 a week. $75 a day.  

Look at it as your 5 year tuition to learn practice and perfect.  

Are you trading to earn a living. Is it a hobby or are you trading solely to get your losses back ? 

Figure out why you’re trading. You can’t make up 5 years in 1 day or 1 week.  Has it been done in a week or a month SURE. But if we get stuck in a mindset we know is wrong yet are only aware of it after we go against it then the losses are gone and there was no tuition. It was essentially gambling.  

I been there, believe me. I’ve blown $35k in less than 2 months.  I feel your pain. A lot of us do.  

If you make $75 a day and shut her down you will get your losses back in time.  
You’re going to have good days and bad obviously but if your RM is there and you’re actually journaling and going over your journal weekly at least it’s going to make it.  

Anyone can make a trade, we have to find trades.  
All Love Bro. Hope you’re able to get a handle on it all and breathe.

messages.author: Kam
messages.content:
hey <@1323448182851637345>, I'm assuming your trade was a BnR off the 5m opening range. a few points to consider: 

1) on higher timeframes we opened up right in the middle of yesterday's range on the ES, so in order to go long or short at an intraday level like the 5m OR, you ideally need stronger evidence of momentum in either direction. Try zooming out to the 15m or 1hr timeframe to see the bigger picture 

2) the amount of displacement is key for the BnR system. In this case there wasn't a strong push or super strong candle close above the level you're using for the retest. Note the upper wicks (a tell for sellers) on the push above the 5m range on the 1m chart

3) if your entry was on the 9:57 candle, it's likely you didn't wait until the candle closed for confirmation that your level was holding. Remember you want to see strong price action at your level in order to enter. 

Will post a couple of links to videos Jack has made recently that may help with building higher timeframe narratives for your trade ideas as well as good entry confirmation signals

messages.author: Bhargav
messages.content:
From $500 to $7,876 – My Journey of Patience, Discipline, and Belief

Hey everyone,

I wanted to take a moment to share something close to my heart – not to boast, but to hopefully inspire anyone who's feeling stuck or uncertain in their trading journey.

I joined this trading community about a year and a half ago. Back then, I was just eager to learn, hungry for growth, and like many of you, figuring things out one step at a time. It wasn’t always smooth. There were days filled with doubt, losses that made me question everything, and moments when I wondered if this journey was really for me.

But I kept showing up.

I studied, practiced, journaled my trades, learned from my mistakes, and leaned on this community for support and accountability. Every win and every loss taught me something valuable – not just about trading, but about myself.

In June, I decided to take on a personal challenge: turn $500 into $5,000. Not only did I hit that milestone, but I ended the challenge with a $7,876 account.

Yes, the numbers are exciting – but what I’m truly proud of is the mindset it took to get there: patience, consistency, emotional control, and unwavering belief.

This journey has shown me that it’s not about overnight success – it’s about persistence, process, and trusting that your time will come if you don't give up.

To anyone in this community who’s still grinding, still learning, still waiting for that breakthrough: keep going. You’re closer than you think.

Much love and gratitude to all of you who've been part of this path. The journey is far from over — let’s keep growing, together. Big Thanks to <@717554586843611217> , <@801169196015943731> and all the Mentors here.

– Bhargav

messages.author: Brute7
messages.content:
It’s officially been one month since I started my intraday trading journey — and it all began with inspiration from the Scarface YouTube channel. What started as simple curiosity quickly turned into a daily commitment to grow, learn, and show up — regardless of profits or losses.

Like many beginners, I dove into breakout, retest, and price action strategies, thinking I had a solid handle on options trading. But over time, I realized there’s so much more to it — market news, economic reports, index correlations, relative strength, option Greeks, and key levels all play a critical role. One of my early mistakes was spreading my attention too thin, tracking too many tickers without focusing deeply on any.

So three days ago, I made a shift: I committed to just five tickers — SPX, NVDA, TSLA, QQQ, and SPY. Since then, I’ve seen a clear difference. Studying these names daily has helped me understand their behavior better, enter trades with more precision, and exit with more confidence.

Today, I hit my personal all-time high in trading. It’s not $10k (yet), but I know I’m on the right path — one day at a time.

messages.author: John H
messages.content: I don't think someone else's contract size matters. We have to size to our own accounts and the risk we're willing to take with each trade. For example, your risk might be $100 where Jack & Tony may be at $1000.  So, think in terms of if they bought 50 option contracts, they could only lose $.20 before they hit their max loss. If you wanted $.20 of risk equaling $100 then you could only buy 5 contracts. And, $.20 can happen in a split second. That's why knowing where you are getting out, and sizing appropriately to the number of contracts you can buy is important. Your stop might be at $.50, which would then mean only 2 contracts. Hope this makes sense. At the end of the day, it's not apples to apples to try and compare our trade size to what they are doing.

messages.author: Kam
messages.content:
Hey Sean - this is a great question, and one I’ve spent a lot of time thinking about recently.

Honestly, it sounds like you’re doing a lot of the right things already. You’ve built structure around risk, setup clarity, journaling, and daily reflection - really solid foundation.

When I think about consistency, I try to break it down into a few core lenses (credit <@717554586843611217> <@801169196015943731> <@1014389514141184074> and Mark Douglas!)

Are your best setups clearly defined and **repeatable**?

Are you avoiding trades outside your system - even when they “look good”?

Are you managing risk the same way every time - not just when you're confident, but when you're unsure too?

You mentioned you’ve been trading this way for ~6 months. One thing I’ve been using personally is a ‘trading block’ model - aiming for 20 trades aligned with my process out of every 25. It helps me stay honest about process over PnL. If I hit it, I earn the right to pay myself with a small size bump. If not, I reset. It’s been a helpful way to compound responsibly as I've had issues sizing up too quickly in the past. 

25 trades for me typically maps to about a month’s worth of trading, but the calendar isn’t the point - the reps are.

So yeah IMO, it's not about waiting X months or years. It’s about proving to yourself that your process is repeatable. Hope that helps!

messages.author: Hayden
messages.content:
- Candle Close Stop Loss

Pros:

Filters out “wick hunts” and intraday noise.

Keeps you in trades that recover quickly after a fake-out.

Helps align with structure rather than random ticks.

Cons:

Risk can be larger than expected, since by the time the candle closes, the option premium may be down much more.

Hard to execute in options intraday because option premiums are extremely sensitive (gamma, delta shifts). A stock moving $0.30 against you can crush the premium more than expected.

Can lead to hesitating, hoping the candle “saves” the trade, which sometimes just means deeper loss.



- Hard Stop at Exact Price

Pros:

Clear, mechanical, no decision fatigue.

Protects capital better, especially in options where theta + gamma magnify losses quickly.

Removes emotional “what if it comes back?”

Cons:

You’ll get wicked out more often, especially intraday.

Slippage can hurt — a 10–20¢ slip in stock can translate into 20–30% option loss.

Misses some trades that recover immediately after the fake-out.



- Slippage Reality in Options

Options don’t move smoothly with the underlying; spreads + illiquidity + IV shifts = exits are rarely clean.

Market orders at stop get filled worse than stock.

Sometimes you get slipped on size even if spreads look tight.

This is why many intraday options traders actually prefer mental stops or alerts and exit manually, because hard stops can trigger at the worst possible premium.


Simplification: If you hesitate a lot on your stops, you have a small account, use exact stop levels. (Manual or Orders) The BNR usually works right away or it doesn't at all so most of the time (In my opinion) its worth it to close out. Tony and Jack have a lot higher risk tolerance than most and have been trading price action for many years.

If you wait for closures - accept the risk you're R/R might be skewed on a lot of your trades but your win % might be higher (if they reverse), better for experienced traders

messages.author: Jerry
messages.content:
Hey guys,

Wanted to share my experience of going from struggling to profitable (just a few months so far, but still). Maybe it can be a good starting point for some of you—it really changed the game for me.
I struggled a lot before. At one point, I was trading 20 TSLA contracts, taking small wins but then wiping everything out with big losses. Ended up down almost $15k in just a few months. That’s when I stopped and decided to really think.
I built an Excel table with all of Tony’s trades this year, split by symbol. Every row was a trade with entry (I added some delay since I’m slower), stop loss, max potential win until SL was hit, etc. I kept adding parameters and I’m still revisiting them, improving execution.
From that analysis, I found the main thing that killed my profitability: scaling out too early. I used to sell 30% on the HOD pop, then more on the next big candle, and my RR was completely ruined.
So I made a script in Excel to simulate different scenarios and find the best monthly outcome. The result was simple: don’t scale at all until TSLA moves at least $4 (around 2R), then exit. That’s it.
That one change completely flipped my trading. Now I trade 10 contracts and make way more than I did before with 20. This is just the first step, of course. Next is working on dynamic sizing based on setup quality, scaling in/out properly, etc. But this shift was the key—it stopped the bleeding and got me back to making money.

Hope it will help

messages.author: Jerry
messages.content: Yeah exactly. I did the same analysis for amd, Nvidia and appl. Found the profit targets for amd and Nvidia, decided to completely avoid appl, cause the results there (win rate and RR) are quite bad cause good entry and scaling out is essential with this ticker

messages.author: Angel (az)
messages.content: Thanks so much for sharing this, that's super interesting and will be doing a similar analysis as soon as I get a chance!
