But I was second-guessing my scaling lately because like you said, it's so difficult to lose all these trades where Tony/Jack are coming out green or breakeven, makes it feel like the losses are avoidable. And so many are scaling 60-70% at HOD (minus contextual) but I just don't see how that as a base scaling strategy can cover the losses over time and the RR just isn't working out on paper for me. Am super curious how Tony and others manage that, perhaps with the much sharper executions?

messages.author: Jerry
messages.content:
Yeah. Doing your own analysis is essential. Only that way you will get confidence. I simulated different appproaches.
1. No scaling. That gives the best results - best RR and 50% win rate
2. 1 scale. Again tried to find an optimum with simulation where and how many to scale to improve win rate at the price of RR. Managed to do it. Increased to 65% win rate with some combinations but it hit the total profit.
3. 1 scale + runners. Same results as in second approach.

So after all simulations no scaling approach gives the best profit, though much more complicated from mental perspective. 

To the question why Tony manages to have such great result - i see 3 points
1. Much better entry so good RR on HOD
2. Scaling based on good reading of price action like scaling out when several wicks as a sign of weakness
3. Scaling out base on context and not on predefined structure.

But all these points come with experience. We will get there someday

messages.author: G Nice
messages.content: Hey guys, for small accounts (1-2k) or those just starting out. I would highly recommend trading shares instead of options until you have a solid setup/trading plan established. You will have far less anxiety trading shares as it doesn’t move as fast as options (a $1 drop in share price will be a $1 P&L loss/share. where an ATM options contract is typically around $50 loss/con). You can still trade the B&R live with the group and practice your entry, scales and SL as if they are options except it will not blow your account. Do this until you are consistently profitable and have a solid trading plan. The point is not the amount of money you make in shares but mainly to establish a solid scalable trading plan that you can transfer over to options when you are ready. I hate to see/hear people blowing their accounts or losing more than they can manage so I hope this helps.

Thanks for the details - when I was backtesting by myself quite a while back, I was always just around breakeven and I thought it was because I wasn’t identifying the setups correctly but I’ve realized over time it’s most likely this scaling issue, because my win rate tends to match. So scaling is really my last (and big) missing piece. It’s such a good idea to do this scaling analysis on their trades first to eliminate any concerns and variables about my own skill issues in identifying the setup though. 

I think you hit the nail on the head with how Tony can manage the large HOD scales. I’ve been suspecting too that the thing we’re missing is execution speed for better RR, discretion, context and experience. Which are very, very difficult things to quantify and aim to improve other than putting in the time and being patient and then having a bit of faith in our brains to figure it out. So this was a useful reminder that we simply might not be currently ready to scale the same way. 

Thanks for all that, this exact problem has been at the back of my head and bothering me all week and this helped me clarify a lot!

messages.author: 305 Trader
messages.content:
Here is a really sinple method I use as an options trader for scalping,

a)- What is the max loss per trade I am willing to accept today given my account size?

b)- when the set up is forming, I watch where my TPs are, I take a TP1 and also the ultimate, TP1 to me should be 1:2, the ultimate is whatever it can be. Understand this is a super conservative  (but game changer) approach. 

c) - Here is the science, what is the underlying (real stock) $cents size of the most realistic stop loss (not an arbitrary one)??
d)
Formula: 

 c) x contracts number x 0.5 = a). 

Example : Stop loss measures  25 cts of stock value. 
Max loss per trade is $50
Factor is 0.5
So,
25 x 0.5 = 12.5
$50 / 12.5 = 4 contracts. 

That is your contract size to lose $50 as per your measured stop loss (as long as you exit when it says so). 

then, 

Your RvR  ratio to TP1 is 1:2
So,

You have to measure  and find 50 stock value cents to TP1 to enter the trade.

Notice I measure 1:2 RvR to TP1, yes I know this is conservative, but not really cause you are only interested anyways in a  small stop loss, so provided this is what you look for, a RvR to TP1 of 1:2 is realistic and it will occur often. 

Hope this helps, with this method you will be more focused on the  size of your stop loss rather than % of your account to discard set ups that are not winners in what you need: Lower risk

messages.author: Royal191
messages.content: Hi there, we are a learning community first. There are a lot of educational content in here including the accelerator course which is not available on youtube or anywhere else. I would highly recommend you spend some time going through the videos. Feel free to skip the beginner stuff if you like. There are also trade reviews and weekly sessions at your disposal. The live trading is for you to see in real time how the strategy is executed and learn in real time as well. You can follow along and potentially take the same trades as Jack and Tony as they are pretty clear when and why they are entering a trade. They call out which name they are looking at and the levels. But you do need to learn the entry models so you can execute yourself and have a good entry with a better risk to reward. IF you are waiting to hear them say they enter then you will always be a few seconds late which can make a big difference since we are trading momentum and some of these moves happen quickly.

messages.author: Jonathan
messages.content:
All losses teach something. If that NVDA trade still showed a 84% rule after losing a good chunck of % why would you enter again? Personally to me, even if the 84%rule shows itself if my loss has reached a point where i can't mentally handle. I do not enter and just wait for tomorrow. So you not entering that 84% trade can help you depending on how you see it or put into perspective. You already took a big L. Could you have handled another L if that 84%failed? 
Its good to disect every single loss trade and why they failed. 
What was your bias on the past trades you took? Calls or puts?  Ask yourself which ones favored you and which ones didn't. Calls,puts,tickers, time frames, even trading days. All those losing trades mean something about your psychology while trading.

messages.author: Dan Grand
messages.content:
Hey everyone, I wanted to share something important that I realized about my trading journey, and I’m hoping to get some guidance and support from the community.

Back in August, I had an amazing month trading options — one of my best ever. That gave me the confidence to try trading with real money. My first week was +$800, the second week I lost -$1000, and that’s when I realized I didn’t truly understand risk the way I thought I did. I stepped back and tried futures for a while, but trading futures alone without a mentor has been difficult. Watching YouTube isn’t enough when you don’t have someone to ask real questions.

And now, after thinking deeply, I’ve decided not to give up on options. I want to continue trading options seriously, because this is the environment where I feel the most support and structure, and where I’ve already seen my best results. I just want to do it the right way this time.

One big realization I had is this:

I can follow rules.
I can take clean entries.
I can respect my stop.
I can let my trades hit either TP or SL.

But what I cannot do well yet is read the higher-timeframe context and understand when the market wants to move long and when it wants to move short. That’s the piece I’ve never deeply worked on.

Sometimes I take a valid entry according to my rules, but I’m trading it completely against the larger direction — while some of you or the mentors are actually looking for trades in the opposite direction because of context. This made me realize I’m not just “pressing buttons.” I’m missing a whole dimension of the market.

So as I return to trading options, I really want to improve this part of my trading.
If anyone can share advice or help me build a simple, structured plan for learning how to read higher-timeframe context, it would mean a lot.

Thanks in advance for any support or suggestions. I appreciate this community and I’m ready to grow properly this time.

messages.content:
What a great day it was here in the chat 😂
Let’s switch the subject 😉

I want to share how this month is going from my side.
I am BE!

From green to zero — and I actually feel great. Why? Because I feel the progress I’ve made since the moment I found Tony on YouTube and joined this community. I’m still developing my edge, but I stick to my rules. My progress has really accelerated since I got here.

This is where mistakes happen, sometimes good profits happen, and sometimes really good losses happen (meaning even if the trade is bad from an HTF context — which is my weakest point because I never worked on it before — I still respect my SL). And i am barely or even not at all lose money 💰

messages.author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
messages.content:
===========================
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY**

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

**If you’re green for the week**, only trade with a small **PORTION of your profits**. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you’re red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you__**. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

@everyone   

Please confirm you have read this with a 🤑

messages.author: Luke
messages.content:
I agree, it is difficult to get used to quickly calculating risk with the long/short position tool and using delta with it to get an estimate. For me, I always define my risk as a range of price; say I wanted to risk $100-$150 on a trade. Of course with options you can never have an exact amount but you can have a good idea. The delta on the ATM Calls on AMZN today was around .50 delta, and I was looking to risk around .50 on the underlying chart. I would multiply .50 by .50 and get .25 meaning you're risking $25 on each contract. Taking slippage and potentially stopping out a bit below the stop loss, I would take 4 or 5 contracts (risking $100-$140ish).
Hopefully that makes sense, that's just how I've done it and it works well for me

messages.author: DHayesTrades
messages.content: Hey guys - I'm fairly new here been tradig in the community for  afew weeks now. An issue I keep running into is the stop losses. They seem to be fairly discretionary. Today was the perfect example on NVDA. I entered when I saw clear buyers stepping in on the Wednesday Highs level retest - it then reverses and had two candles testing it. Both candles broke that level (where my stop loss was) but both candles did not close underneath. I stopped out when it broke. The trade then runs amazingly without me lol. Cand someone please inform me how to successfully place and respect the stop losses around these key levels especially during a candle trying to break it?

messages.author: JP400
messages.content:
Mess up today. Took AMD today PMH at 10:01 with 2 Cons. Looking for 2:1. Stop at PMH. Did not get the 2:1. Got scared when it came back and sold 1 Con to minimize loss. Then it stepped up again, so I bought another. Sold 1 Con at the close of 10:05. Pushed again. Held for 2:1. Did not get it. Then crossed my fingers (that well known strategy). Then sold last Con at 10:13 with QQQ selling too.
Tried AMD again at 10:14... (with QQQ above PDH). Lost again.
Tried AMD again at 10:22... (with QQQ above PDH). Lost again.
Then recovered some on 2 QQQ Puts off PML at 10:57. Caught that drop. Nice. And recovered my losses. Green day.
But I didn't stop trading !
I bought 2 QQQ again at 11:05 for a drop to PDL.  But it reversed, and I paid the price on the fast price changes on Friday Puts. Wiped out more gains.
And then... Took 2 more QQQ trades (2 Cons each time) to try to recover. Clawed some back. But just reinforcing terrible habits !
Overall loss on the day.

Sigh... Too tired after a busy day repairing cars yesterday. Wanted to make some money. Forgot the No.1 rule that it's not about making money. Broke my 11am rule, and did not stop at 11am. And also took waaaaaay too many trades.
Sigh...

messages.author: Jdub
messages.content:
Biggest Day Of The Year For Me!!

+12K Realized Intraday Plus Some Trailers Still
+17K Unrealized In Long Term 🙂

Beautiful trade today on TSLA. Hopefully some of you guys were able to capture it. I try my very best to outline the moves before hand in the pre market and in the live sessions. The reason I do them is for you guys to build conviction yourself. There is no better feeling then having your own conviction in a trade. That's why we always preach understanding whats going on instead of relying on "signals". You can follow signals all day but in the end you won't become profitable if don't learn to trade for yourself.

With that being said I just took some time to reflect today and I wanted to let you guys know that trading is hard. We might make it seem easier then it is at times but the reality is that trading is one of the most difficult professions in the world. 

I started trading in 2019. At the time I was living with my grandparents in an unfinished basement. I was in school, was working at Amazon while trying to make trading work. For the first two years I was losing every-single day and it felt like I couldn't even put on a winning trade. Nothing felt like it was going my way. I was struggling everyday but I knew that it was only a matter of time. 

I know some of you guys are in that position right now and I just want to let you guys know that it is going to get better. Everything that you need to succeed in the markets is all in front of you. All you have to do is put in the work. Show up to the pre markets preps, watch the live sessions over and over again, watch the trade reviews. We can layout the full blue print but in the end it is up to YOU to put in the work. I can't come and click the buttons for you. 

Just wanted to end it off here. I usually don't get too emotional or like typing out these motivational speeches but I thought if it can help someone or motivate someone then it would be worth it.  At the end of the day trading is very lonely and I think we've built a great community here and all want each other to win. 

Let's keep killing it team! @everyone

messages.author: MambaTrades
messages.content:
One of my biggest issues the past 2-3 weeks of trading was being married to my daily bias and not being adaptive to change in price action and volume. 

For example, we've had a lot of gap downs and gap fills up and I kept trying to short the move up because I was married with the higher timeframe bias/narrative. Not good for this current market conditions with all the news, tariffs, and dip buyers. 

Moral of the story is be flexible and be willing to adapt your bias if price tells you a different story. 

Have a great trading day @here

messages.author: TonyMontana
messages.content:
I know some people are talking about FOMO right now.

Here’s a perspective.

For myself on Friday I was estatic (ofc I hit a $130k trade) biggest of my career.

But guess what?

The stock ran another 11-12% today.

With the trailers I had this was an extra $25-35k if I rolled them into this week.

If I held my full position (NOT SAYING I WOULD AT ALL) it would be a $1mill + swing.

Think about that.

However, I don’t care.

Why?

Our job as traders is NOT to catch the whole move.

It’s to catch the the highest probability chunk.

I was able to do this and document it for you guys.

But no matter if you’re the best trader in the world you will have feelings and you will NOT catch all the moves.

Observe, learn and refine that’s all we can do.

Hope this helps some people from not full porting to chase TSLA gains.

@everyone

messages.content:
I lost $30k trying to learn trading.

It was my full networth 5 years ago.

Blessed to be able to call $30k just another week now.

As a new trader start with SMALL size, LEARN the skill (your in the right community), and most importantly embrace the losses as lessons.

It can be hard seeing others win when you’re losing.

It can also be hard remaining grounded when you’re winning.

In this room our message is clear:

We are NOT a signal service but we are an educational community. 

We want traders to come in and SEE how we view the markets, not to just enter because we are.

With this being said, these last couple months we have seen fantastic results and I’m glad you are able to see both the WINS & LOSSES in real time.

Hope yall had a good week.

I’ll catch you in the trade review tonight.

The goal for 2025 was to size up.

Why?

Now, the results you see were built over the last 7 years.

Stay small, learn the skill, once you master it then and only then size up.

This applies in trading, business or any other avenue.

It’s important to remember.

You guys see the good & bad.

This is the SAME trade as yesterday we took on GOOGL.

No.

I said yesterday it was a good setup, probability just didn’t work and hopefully today you see that.

I always say this:

process focused traders will make more in the long run than result focused traders.

messages.author: CBD Steve
messages.content:
NVDA Earnings plan - Fake out candle has happened last 3 quarters systematically between 4:10pm-4:20pm dropping $6-$9.

November, 20th 2024 - Fake out 10 minute candle: $137.92 - $146.24 (Good sell time 40 minutes after fake out) 9:20 price is $149.62

February, 26th 2025 - Fake out 10 minute candle: $125.58 - $136.59 (Ideal sell time 40 minutes after fake out) 9:20 price is $135.42 not higher than 40 minutes after

May, 28th 2025 - Fake out 10 minute candle: $133.29 - 139.99 (Ideal sell time 40 minutes after fake out) 9:20 price is $142.73 not higher than 40 minutes after

My plan: Wait for fake out to bottom out - confirm with volume chart. Buy 100 shares of NVDA. Butt clench for 40 minutes. 4:50-4:59pm sell all shares.

Fake out will drop $7-$10 historically (3 quarters data)

Buy price - only once confirmed reversal
Risk - Extremely high (no stops)
Reward - $500-$1000 profit on 100 shares
Plan B - Hold shares for 1-3 weeks if it needs to recover 
Research - About an hr lol
Additional help - I could only go back on earnings based on 10 minute candle so there is more I could be missing
Additional Discovery - At 4am 1 day before earnings it gaps up 1-3%. 2-3 quarters. It did gap down 1 quarter.

messages.author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
messages.content:
Hi Kam, what would be your advice on "intuitively bad setups" during backtesting? 

For example, if I feel like the ticker is choppy today, I get one loss in a long position during the first 30 min after market open, then it reverses and shows an entry into a short position. But I personally wouldn't take it based on my experience with this kind of choppy days. 

Do I have to still take it during backtesting, because this "feeling" of mine is barely repeatable?
And what do I do if most of the losses in backtesting come from this kind of setups that somehow look bad but I don't always understand why.

messages.author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
messages.content:
Thanks a lot Kam! That's very thorough. 

Yeah that's kinda what I'm doing, trying to notice common traits in these "off setups" and create hypothesis based on them for further backtesting. Like, not taking setups under HoD / above LoD which filters most of the chop in range also killing some good setups, but overall more efficient.

It's harder with setups where I don't see anything objectively different. For example, if the structure is very stiff, still keeping it bullish or bearish, but the PA looks unnatural, popping and dropping very hard. It's like, I think to myself, that's not what I usually see when I get a good setup, but this is sooo subjective and also hard to repeat in real time trading.

But yeah, I got what you mean, thanks a lot!

messages.author: DWalt Trades
messages.content:
Opinion based answer:

Hi this was a good trade with a well managed risk 🙂👍 Technically, you were never invalidated, but I can totally see why you closed this one. Shorting QQQ (stronger index rn) right into a key level, with no new LOD, and looking like PML was gonna be defended while taking time decay, you cut the trade and there is nothing wrong with managing your risk because in real time you can only react to what is on the left, and not predict what is coming on the right. 

I agree with what you said in your review: Right Idea, Wrong Entry. — But this one could’ve definitely been given a little more room potentially risking off that ORL reclaim — judging by the 5M chart.

What you could have done differently IMO: Not shorting the stronger index. QQQ at the time of your entry had a LOT of intraday strength over the SPY. With that said you would be better off shorting SPY today and using QQQ as downside confluence.

I added the chart of what this same trade would’ve looked like on the weaker side with the SPY PUTS, which also offered a 2.48 RR @ LOD compared to QQQ’s 1.63 RR @ LOD — based on your entry and exit here.

messages.author: Luke
messages.content: When you start backtesting as a beginner to the system,  the first thing you want to do is backtest the specific setups to build confidence in the specific ones (Previous Day High, Premarket High, 5min high, 1 Candle Rule etc..); you probably have heard that there is no "best setup" in terms of the level, but instead it's all based on context and HTFs as well. But once you backtest the specific setups just to build confidence in them and to keep it simple, what I would do is start marking out levels like you would for one ticker and backtest that one for the day with any setup that sets-up. Then, this builds confidence in your edge... and helps you to know that your system actually wins, but also loses under certain conditions. This helps with getting to know personalities of tickers as well. What I currently do is go to random days, and mark out basic levels on all of the tickers on my watchlist, and play what looks best on the HTFs; I'll draw arrows like my Premarket Gameplan and treat it like real-time... if you use the 1 second interval on 1 second candles, it makes it very realistic to taking the trade in real time (of course emotions aren't there). One thing I highly recommend you do is go to the live trading on a day that you backtested and took a hypothetical trade, and see if Tony and Jack were looking at it, or even took it (then if they did you can go watch the trade review as well). Overall, these are super solid ways to boost your confidence, and help you to know especially on losing streaks that your edge works over a long period of time. Hope this all makes sense... let me know if you have any questions!

messages.author: Sean
messages.content:
Decided to test out PDH/PDL on SPY for the month of Feb 2025. This is the result so far. 
This is just looking for a Break of the PDH or PDL selling 50% at HOD/LOD or 1:1R and continued selling as price approached 2R+.
Stops are at points of invalidation. Usually a break above/below candle entry or PDH/L level. 
I'm also stopping out if 1st TP is hit and retraces back to BE. 
I'm going to continue backtesting this one strat on one ticker for the rest of the year and see how it goes. So far so good.

messages.author: kalerv
messages.content:
Hey, thanks for the detailed feedback — genuinely useful to hear how other traders are thinking about this.

Before jumping into building, I want to make sure anything added actually improves decisions rather than just adding more to look at. A few honest questions:

On multiple benchmarks: When SPY and QQQ are pointing in different directions on a given day, what do you actually do differently? Do you trade both signals, pick the stronger one, or sit out entirely? Asking because two RS columns that sometimes disagree might create more confusion than clarity — want to understand your actual process before wiring it in.

On ORB timeframe: What timeframe do you use and why that one specifically? And when price is sitting between the ORB high and low, do you pass on the trade or just note it as context? If it’s a hard filter for you, that’s worth building. If it’s just nice to see, it probably doesn’t need its own column.

On VWAP/EMA: Can you walk me through a recent trade where VWAP or an EMA specifically changed your decision — not just confirmed it, but actually changed it? The concern here is that these are session-anchor or HTF references, and this tool is designed to find intraday relative strength right now. Adding HTF filters risks answering a different question than the one the tool is built for.

On filtering: Agreed it sounds useful, but a stock that’s “Inside” at 9:40 and filtered out might be your best trade at 10:15. The color coding already lets you scan for what meets criteria without hiding names you might need later.

The core goal of this tool is one thing: at any point during the session, which names are genuinely leading or lagging the market right now — not just historically, not just structurally, but in the tape. Everything in scope needs to serve that question directly.

What does your actual pre-trade checklist look like step by step? That would help figure out where this fits and what, if anything, is genuinely missing.
